Masculine Declension (Κλίση Αρσενικών)

Let's look at the "cheat codes" for Masculine nouns.

Almost all masculine nouns end in -ος, -ας, or -ης. These are the three families of masculine words.

Remember from our lesson on Cases: you only need to worry about the Nominative (Subject) and the Accusative (Object) for now. The Genitive (Possession) is included in the tables below for future reference.

1. Nouns ending in -ος

This is the biggest and most famous family of masculine words. Example: ο φίλος (the friend)

Case Singular English
Nom (Subject) ο φίλος The friend is here.
Acc (Object) το(ν) φίλο I see the friend.
Gen (Owner) του φίλου The car of the friend.

What happens? When the friend becomes the object (Accusative), two things happen: 1. The article ο changes to τον. 2. The noun φίλος drops its final -ς and becomes φίλο.

Wait, why is it "τον" sometimes and "το" other times? This is a pronunciation rule. You keep the 'N' if the next word starts with a vowel or a hard consonant (κ, π, τ, μπ, ντ, γκ, τσ, τζ, ξ, ψ). Otherwise, you drop the 'N'. Don't worry about memorizing this yet!

2. Nouns ending in -ας

Example: ο πατέρας (the father)

Case Singular
Nom (Subject) ο πατέρας
Acc (Object) το(ν) πατέρα
Gen (Owner) του πατέρα

What happens? It's the exact same rule! The article becomes τον, and the noun drops its final -ς.

[!TIP] The "S" Rule The golden rule for almost all masculine nouns in the singular: If it's the subject, it keeps the 'S'. If it's receiving the action (Object/Accusative) or if it's the owner (Genitive), it drops the 'S'.

3. Nouns ending in -ης

Example: ο χάρτης (the map)

Case Singular
Nom (Subject) ο χάρτης
Acc (Object) το(ν) χάρτη
Gen (Owner) του χάρτη

What happens? You guessed it! The article changes to τον and the word drops its -ς.

The Plural Forms

When you want to talk about multiple friends/fathers/maps, the endings change again to show Plurality.

Ending Plural Nominative (Subjects) Plural Accusative (Objects)
-ος οι φίλοι (the friends) τους φίλους (the friends)
-ας οι πατέρες (the fathers) τους πατέρες (the fathers)
-ης οι χάρτες (the maps) τους χάρτες (the maps)

[!WARNING] Notice that for the -ας and -ης groups, the plural forms are exactly the same whether they are subjects or objects! - Οι πατέρες είναι εδώ. (The fathers are here). - Βλέπω τους πατέρες. (I see the fathers). The only thing that changes is the article (οι ➔ τους).

🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist

  • [ ] I know that masculine words almost always drop their final -ς when they are the Object (Accusative).
  • [ ] I know that the article ο changes to τον.
  • [ ] I know the plural article for subjects is οι, and for objects is τους.
